stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents to the ED with 3 hours of substernal chest pressure radiating to her left arm. She is diaphoretic. Vitals: BP 165/95, HR 102, SpO2 96% on RA. ECG shows ST elevations in leads II, III, aVF. Troponin pending. Current medications: metformin 1000mg BID, lisinopril 20mg daily, atorvastatin 40mg daily.

A 45-year-old man presents with 2 weeks of progressive fatigue, polyuria, and polydipsia. He has lost 15 pounds unintentionally. No significant past medical history. BMI 22. Vitals normal. Fasting glucose 342 mg/dL. BMP notable for Na 131, K 5.2, bicarb 18, BUN 28, Cr 1.4. Urinalysis shows 3+ glucose, moderate ketones.

A 78-year-old woman with CHF (EF 30%), CKD stage 3b (GFR 38), and atrial fibrillation on warfarin presents with 5 days of worsening dyspnea, orthopnea, and bilateral lower extremity edema. She ran out of furosemide 1 week ago. Vitals: BP 148/92, HR 88 irregularly irregular, RR 24, SpO2 91% on RA. JVP elevated, bibasilar crackles, 3+ pitting edema. BNP 2,400. INR 3.8. Cr 2.1 (baseline 1.6). K 5.6.
critical_safety: Digoxin level check if on digoxin

stem: A 35-year-old previously healthy woman presents with 10 days of fever (Tmax 102.4F), night sweats, a 10-pound weight loss, and a new rash. She recently returned from a 3-week trip to rural Southeast Asia. On exam: diffuse maculopapular rash, hepatosplenomegaly, generalized lymphadenopathy. Labs: WBC 2.8 (low), Hgb 10.2, Plt 98, AST 145, ALT 132, LDH 890. Peripheral smear shows atypical lymphocytes.
chief_complaint: Fever, rash, weight loss after travel
critical_safety: Thick and thin malaria smears STAT
HIV RNA viral load (not just antibody)
Avoid NSAIDs if dengue suspected (hemorrhagic risk)

stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of alcohol use disorder (6-8 beers daily for 20 years) presents with 3 days of confusion, asterixis, and jaundice. He stopped drinking 5 days ago. Vitals: BP 105/68, HR 112, T 100.8F. Exam: scleral icterus, spider angiomata, ascites, asterixis. Labs: total bilirubin 12.4, direct 8.2, AST 210, ALT 85 (AST:ALT >2), INR 2.4, albumin 2.1, Cr 2.8 (baseline 0.9), Na 126. Ammonia 142. Paracentesis: WBC 620 (85% PMNs), protein 1.1, culture pending.
critical_safety: Do NOT give benzodiazepines with hepatic encephalopathy unless confirmed withdrawal

stem: A 68-year-old man with COPD on home O2 (2L baseline), HTN, and BPH presents with 4 days of productive cough with green sputum, worsening dyspnea, and wheezing. He has used his albuterol inhaler 8 times today. Vitals: BP 155/88, HR 105, RR 28, SpO2 87% on 2L NC, T 101.2F. Exam: diffuse expiratory wheezing, accessory muscle use, prolonged expiratory phase. ABG on 2L: pH 7.31, pCO2 58, pO2 55, HCO3 28. CXR: hyperinflation, right lower lobe infiltrate.
critical_safety: Monitor for CO2 narcosis with O2 escalation
Avoid high-flow O2 in chronic CO2 retainer

stem: A 42-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presents with 6 weeks of progressive bilateral joint pain and stiffness (hands, wrists, knees), morning stiffness lasting >2 hours, fatigue, and a 5-pound weight loss. On exam: symmetric swelling of MCP and PIP joints bilaterally, warmth at wrists, no nodules. Labs: ESR 68, CRP 4.2, RF positive (titer 1:320), anti-CCP positive (>250 U/mL), ANA positive (1:160 speckled), CBC normal, CMP normal, UA normal.
critical_safety: Baseline hepatitis B/C screening before DMARD initiation
Do not delay DMARD initiation if RA confirmed (treat-to-target)
Avoid prolonged NSAID monotherapy as primary treatment

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with 3 episodes of severe, throbbing headache over the past month, each lasting 4-72 hours, accompanied by nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. She describes a 20-minute visual aura (zigzag lines) before two of the episodes. She has been taking ibuprofen 800mg 3-4 times per week for the past 2 months. PMH: depression (on sertraline 100mg daily). She uses combined oral contraceptives. Family history: mother has migraines.
critical_safety: STOP combined oral contraceptives (migraine with aura = contraindication due to stroke risk)
NSAID overuse causing medication overuse headache (>10 days/month)
Triptans contraindicated with concurrent SSRI? (serotonin syndrome risk — FDA warning, though clinical risk is low)
MRI brain if first presentation of aura to exclude structural cause

stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of hypertension, diabetes, and stage 4 CKD (GFR 22) presents for routine follow-up. Current medications: amlodipine 10mg, losartan 100mg, metformin 500mg BID, insulin glargine 20 units, atorvastatin 80mg, aspirin 81mg. Labs: K 5.8, Cr 3.2 (baseline 2.8), BUN 48, bicarb 16, phosphorus 6.2, calcium 8.1, Hgb 9.2, A1c 8.4%. BP today: 152/88.
chief_complaint: Routine CKD follow-up with lab abnormalities
expected_ddx: Uncontrolled diabetes and hypertension accelerating CKD
critical_safety: Consider holding or reducing losartan (contributes to hyperkalemia + AKI risk)
Nephrology referral for dialysis access planning if not already established

stem: A 58-year-old woman presents to the ED with sudden onset of right-sided weakness and slurred speech that began 90 minutes ago. Her husband reports she was fine when she woke up but became symptomatic while eating breakfast. PMH: atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation — declined warfarin 6 months ago), hypertension, hyperlipidemia. Vitals: BP 185/105, HR 92 irregularly irregular, SpO2 98%. Neuro exam: left gaze preference, right facial droop, right arm drift (4-/5), right leg 4/5, dysarthria. NIHSS: 12. CT head: no hemorrhage.
chief_complaint: Acute right-sided weakness and slurred speech
expected_ddx: Acute ischemic stroke (cardioembolic, likely from AF)
critical_safety: Blood pressure management per AHA guidelines (permissive HTN, lower only if >185/110 for tPA)
Do NOT give aspirin within 24 hours of tPA
CT angiography for large vessel occlusion → thrombectomy evaluation
Start anticoagulation for AF after 24-48 hours post-tPA (not immediately)
